front: Ruler: fourth king of the Joseon Dynasty, also known by the name Yi Do or the title Grand Prince Chungnyeong
Ruler: was the third son of King Taejong, whom he succeeded on the Joseon throne
Event: scholars in this ruler's Hall of Worthies produced the Hunminjeongeum manual, promulgating a new alphabet meant to improve commoner literacy
work: Hangul and the Hall of Worthies

front: Event: this ruler's new alphabet was said to be learnable by "a wise man ... in a morning, and a stupid one in ten days"
work: Hangul and the Hall of Worthies

front: Event: this ruler's new writing system was a 28-character alphabet designed for ease of learning, partially replacing Chinese-derived hanja characters
work: Hangul and the Hall of Worthies

front: Work: a 125-canto epic commissioned by this ruler that glorifies six of his royal ancestors
back: Song of the Dragons Flying to Heaven (Sejong the Great)
work: Hangul and the Hall of Worthies

front: Event: a scientist of low-born status elevated by this ruler invented the Cheugugi, an early standardized bronze rain gauge measuring precipitation in "poon" units
Event: this ruler's scientist Jang Yeong-sil improved on Choe Yun-ui's earlier work to create the Gabinja, an advanced bronze movable-type printing press
Event: Jang Yeong-sil fell from this ruler's favor after a sedan chair he built for the king broke while transporting him
Event: this ruler reformed the country's map system to place the capital at the center, leading to more accurate astronomical predictions
Law: this ruler ran a five-month public opinion poll of roughly 17,000 people before implementing a new agricultural tax
Law: this ruler granted extended maternity and paternity leave to members of the enslaved nobi class
Work: an agricultural manual sponsored by this ruler that accompanied a push for standardized rain gauges
back: Straight Talk on Farming (Sejong the Great)

front: Law: this ruler instituted the "Peasants Relocation Policy" (samin) to resettle newly conquered northern territory near the Yalu River
Institution: this ruler's Stabilization Office introduced a cash coin to replace officially stamped cotton currency
Event: this ruler launched a 1419 campaign against wokou pirates based on Tsushima Island, controlled by the So clan, on his father Taejong's orders
work: Military Campaigns: the Oei Invasion and the Jurchen Frontier

front: Treaty: after the Oei Invasion, this ruler granted the So family trading privileges under this agreement
work: Military Campaigns: the Oei Invasion and the Jurchen Frontier

front: Event: this ruler established a series of four forts and six military outposts to counteract the Jurchen
work: Military Campaigns: the Oei Invasion and the Jurchen Frontier

front: Event: this ruler's second son, Sejo, staged a coup against this ruler's grandson, King Danjong; opponents of the coup included the Six Martyred Ministers, originally appointed by this ruler
back: Six Martyred Ministers / Sejo's coup (Sejong the Great)
work: Succession Crisis: the Six Martyred Ministers

front: Event: this ruler banished two prospective princesses for his heir, one accused of witchcraft and the other of having an affair with a lowborn maid
work: Succession Crisis: the Six Martyred Ministers

front: Event: a massive statue of this ruler stands next to Gyeongbokgung Palace in Seoul
Event: this ruler lends his name to an Aegis-enabled KDX Phase III class of naval destroyers
Event: a modern think tank was named after this ruler following the failed Rangoon Bombing
Event: this ruler promoted Confucianism as state ideology, initially suppressing Buddhism before later tolerating it
Law: this ruler issued a decree banning the practice of Islam by his realm's predominantly Turkic "huihui" community
